title: HTTP, REST & Web API Fundamentals
summary: How HTTP methods, status codes, REST conventions, statelessness, content negotiation, and CORS form the backbone of system-to-system communication.
content:
HTTP is the request/response protocol that most embassy integrations ride on. A client sends a request (method + URL + headers + optional body) and the server returns a response (status code + headers + optional body). REST (Representational State Transfer) is an architectural style layered on HTTP that models the system as resources (nouns) addressed by URLs, manipulated with standard HTTP methods (verbs).

The core methods map to CRUD: POST=Create, GET=Read, PUT/PATCH=Update, DELETE=Delete. Two properties matter for reliability. SAFE means the method does not change server state (GET, HEAD, OPTIONS are safe). IDEMPOTENT means repeating the same request has the same effect as doing it once (GET, PUT, DELETE, HEAD are idempotent; POST and PATCH are generally NOT). PUT replaces a whole resource; PATCH applies a partial update. This is why a retried PUT is safe but a retried POST may create duplicates.

• Status codes signal outcome by class: 2xx success, 3xx redirect, 4xx client error, 5xx server error.
• 200 OK (success with body), 201 Created (new resource, usually with a Location header), 204 No Content (success, empty body — common for DELETE/PUT).
• 301 Moved Permanently vs 302 Found (temporary redirect).
• 400 Bad Request (malformed), 401 Unauthorized (not authenticated), 403 Forbidden (authenticated but not allowed), 404 Not Found, 429 Too Many Requests (rate limited — honor Retry-After).
• 500 Internal Server Error (server bug), 503 Service Unavailable (overloaded/down — retryable).

REST resource naming uses plural nouns and hierarchy, not verbs: GET /embassies/42/visa-applications (good) rather than GET /getVisaApps?id=42 (bad). The HTTP method already supplies the verb. Use path segments for identity and query strings for filtering, sorting, and pagination (e.g. ?status=pending&page=2).

Statelessness is a defining REST constraint: each request must carry everything the server needs (auth token, parameters); the server keeps no client session between requests. This makes APIs horizontally scalable — any server instance can handle any request — which is essential when traffic is load-balanced across data centers.

Content negotiation lets client and server agree on a representation. The client sends Accept: application/json (what it wants) and Content-Type: application/json (what it is sending); the server replies with its own Content-Type. The same resource can be served as JSON, XML, or CSV depending on Accept. Accept-Language and Accept-Encoding negotiate locale and compression similarly.

CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing) is a browser security mechanism. By default the same-origin policy blocks a web page from calling an API on a different origin (scheme+host+port). The server opts in by returning Access-Control-Allow-Origin and related headers; for non-simple requests the browser first sends a preflight OPTIONS request. CORS is enforced by browsers only — it is not a server-side firewall and does not protect server-to-server calls.
keyPoints: Methods map to CRUD: POST=Create, GET=Read, PUT/PATCH=Update, DELETE=Delete.
Safe = no state change (GET); idempotent = repeat is harmless (GET/PUT/DELETE). POST and PATCH are not idempotent.
PUT replaces the full resource; PATCH applies a partial update.
Status classes: 2xx success, 3xx redirect, 4xx client error, 5xx server error.
401 = not authenticated; 403 = authenticated but not authorized; 404 = not found; 429 = rate limited (honor Retry-After).
201 Created returns a Location header; 204 No Content has an empty body.
REST URLs use plural nouns and hierarchy, never verbs — the HTTP method is the verb.
Statelessness: every request is self-contained; the server stores no per-client session, enabling horizontal scaling.
Content negotiation: Accept = what the client wants; Content-Type = what the message body is.
CORS is browser-enforced same-origin protection that the server opts out of with Access-Control-Allow-* headers; it does not secure server-to-server traffic.

flashcards.back: GET, PUT, DELETE, HEAD, OPTIONS. POST and PATCH are generally NOT idempotent — retrying them can cause duplicates or unintended changes.

Safe = the method causes no server state change (GET, HEAD). Idempotent = making the request many times has the same effect as once (GET, PUT, DELETE). All safe methods are idempotent, but not vice versa (PUT is idempotent but not safe).
PUT replaces the entire resource with the supplied representation (idempotent). PATCH applies a partial/incremental update to specific fields (not guaranteed idempotent).
Create=POST, Read=GET, Update=PUT (full) or PATCH (partial), Delete=DELETE.
401 Unauthorized = the caller is not authenticated (no/invalid credentials). 403 Forbidden = the caller is authenticated but lacks permission for this resource.

flashcards.front: What does 429 mean and how should a client react?
flashcards.back: 429 Too Many Requests = the client is being rate-limited. The client should back off and retry, ideally after the time given in the Retry-After header.
200 OK = success with a response body. 201 Created = a new resource was created (usually with a Location header pointing to it). 204 No Content = success with no body (common for DELETE/PUT).
500 Internal Server Error = an unexpected server-side failure (often a bug). 503 Service Unavailable = the server is temporarily overloaded or down — generally retryable, may include Retry-After.
301 Moved Permanently = the resource has a new permanent URL (clients/caches should update). 302 Found = temporary redirect; keep using the original URL.

flashcards.front: Good vs bad REST URL design
flashcards.back: Good: GET /visa-applications/42 (plural noun + id, verb implied by method). Bad: GET /getVisaApplication?id=42 (verb in path). Use query params for filtering/sorting/pagination.

flashcards.front: What does statelessness mean in REST?
flashcards.back: Each request carries all the information needed to process it (e.g. auth token); the server keeps no client session between requests, enabling any instance to handle any request and easy horizontal scaling.
Accept = the representation format the client wants back (e.g. Accept: application/json). Content-Type = the format of the body actually being sent in this message.

The browser same-origin policy blocks a page from calling an API on a different origin. CORS lets the server opt in via Access-Control-Allow-Origin (and a preflight OPTIONS for non-simple requests). It is enforced by browsers only — not a server firewall.

flashcards.front: Why is a retried POST risky but a retried PUT safe?
flashcards.back: POST is not idempotent, so retrying after an uncertain failure can create a duplicate resource. PUT is idempotent — applying it again yields the same final state. Use idempotency keys to make POST safe to retry.

title: API Styles, Authentication & Data Formats
summary: Comparing REST, SOAP, GraphQL, and gRPC; the main authentication models (API keys, OAuth2, JWT, OIDC, mTLS, HMAC); and the data formats and encodings that travel between systems.
content:
Different integration jobs call for different API styles. REST over JSON is the default for public, web-facing APIs — simple, cacheable, and stateless. SOAP is an older XML-based protocol described by a WSDL contract; it is verbose but offers strong typing, built-in standards (WS-Security), and is still common in government, banking, and legacy embassy back-office systems. GraphQL exposes a single endpoint with a typed schema and lets the client request exactly the fields it needs in one query, avoiding over- and under-fetching. gRPC uses HTTP/2 and Protocol Buffers (a compact binary, schema-defined format) for fast, strongly-typed service-to-service calls; it is excellent for internal microservices but is not browser-native.

• REST = resources + HTTP verbs, text JSON, broad tooling.
• SOAP = WSDL contract, XML envelopes, heavyweight, enterprise/legacy.
• GraphQL = one endpoint, client-specified fields, solves over/under-fetching.
• gRPC = HTTP/2 + Protobuf binary, low-latency internal microservices.

Authentication answers who are you; authorization answers what may you do. The simplest credential is an API key — a static shared secret sent in a header. It is easy but coarse-grained and hard to scope or rotate. OAuth2 is the standard delegated-authorization framework issuing short-lived access tokens. Two flows dominate: the authorization-code flow is for user-facing apps where a human consents and the app acts on the user's behalf; the client-credentials flow is for machine-to-machine integration with no user, where a service authenticates with its own client ID and secret. A JWT (JSON Web Token) is a common token format: a signed, base64url-encoded JSON object (header.payload.signature) that carries claims and can be verified without a database lookup.

OpenID Connect (OIDC) is an identity layer built on top of OAuth2. OAuth2 alone handles authorization (delegated access); OIDC adds authentication by issuing an ID token proving who the user is. mTLS (mutual TLS) authenticates both sides of a connection with X.509 certificates — the client proves its identity to the server and vice versa — and is favored for high-assurance, system-to-system links between trusted parties. For inbound webhooks, the sender computes an HMAC signature over the payload using a shared secret and puts it in a header; the receiver recomputes the HMAC and compares, verifying both authenticity and integrity (the message came from the real sender and was not tampered with).

Data formats are how meaning is serialized on the wire. JSON is the lightweight, human-readable default for REST. XML is verbose but supports schemas (XSD) for strict validation and namespaces — central to SOAP. CSV is a flat tabular format good for bulk exports/imports but with no nesting and weak typing. Base64 encodes binary data (files, certificates, images) as ASCII text so it can be embedded inside JSON/XML; note it inflates size by roughly one third and is encoding, not encryption.

Two conventions prevent painful integration bugs. Always use UTF-8 text encoding so names and characters from any language transmit correctly — critical for an embassy handling many alphabets. Always exchange timestamps in UTC, ideally ISO 8601 with a Z suffix (e.g. 2026-06-20T14:30:00Z), and convert to local time only for display; storing local times without offsets is a frequent source of errors. MIME types (media types) such as application/json, application/xml, text/csv, and application/pdf label the kind of content so the receiver parses it correctly; they appear in Content-Type and Accept headers.
keyPoints: REST/JSON is the web default; SOAP/WSDL is XML-based and common in legacy/government systems.
GraphQL uses one endpoint and lets clients request exactly the fields they need, solving over/under-fetching.
gRPC uses HTTP/2 + Protocol Buffers (binary, schema-defined) for fast internal microservice calls; not browser-native.
Authentication = who you are; authorization = what you may do.
OAuth2 authorization-code flow = user-delegated (a human consents); client-credentials flow = machine-to-machine with no user.
A JWT is a signed base64url JSON token (header.payload.signature) carrying verifiable claims.
OIDC adds an authentication/identity layer (ID token) on top of OAuth2's authorization.
mTLS authenticates both client and server via X.509 certificates for high-assurance links.
Webhook security uses HMAC: sender signs the payload with a shared secret, receiver recomputes and compares for authenticity + integrity.
Use UTF-8 for text and UTC (ISO 8601 with Z) for timestamps; base64 turns binary into text and is encoding, not encryption.

flashcards.back: REST is a lightweight architectural style using HTTP verbs and (usually) JSON. SOAP is a heavier XML protocol with a formal WSDL contract and built-in standards like WS-Security — common in legacy/enterprise/government systems.
Over-fetching and under-fetching. A single typed endpoint lets the client request exactly the fields it needs in one query, instead of calling several fixed REST endpoints or getting unwanted data.

flashcards.front: What is gRPC and when is it used?
flashcards.back: A high-performance RPC framework over HTTP/2 using Protocol Buffers (compact binary, schema-defined). Ideal for low-latency internal microservice-to-microservice calls; not natively callable from browsers.
Protobuf — a language-neutral binary serialization format defined by a .proto schema. Smaller and faster to parse than JSON/XML; used by gRPC and requires both sides to share the schema.
Authentication (authn) verifies identity — who you are. Authorization (authz) verifies permissions — what you are allowed to do. OIDC handles authn; OAuth2 handles authz.
An API key is a static shared secret — simple but coarse, hard to scope/rotate. OAuth2 is a framework issuing short-lived, scoped access tokens, supporting user consent and revocation.

Authorization-code: a user is present and consents; the app acts on the user's behalf. Client-credentials: no user — a service authenticates with its own client ID/secret for machine-to-machine access.

flashcards.front: What is a JWT and how is it verified?
flashcards.back: A JSON Web Token: header.payload.signature, base64url-encoded. It carries claims and is verified by checking the signature with a key — no database lookup needed (self-contained/stateless).

flashcards.front: What does OpenID Connect add to OAuth2?
flashcards.back: An identity/authentication layer. OAuth2 grants delegated authorization; OIDC adds an ID token that proves the user's identity (who they are).
Mutual TLS — both client and server present and validate X.509 certificates, so each authenticates the other. Used for high-assurance, trusted system-to-system connections.

flashcards.front: How is a webhook verified with HMAC?
flashcards.back: The sender computes an HMAC of the payload using a shared secret and sends it in a header. The receiver recomputes the HMAC over the received body and compares — confirming authenticity (real sender) and integrity (untampered).
JSON is lightweight, human-readable, the REST default. XML is verbose but supports schemas (XSD) for strict validation and namespaces; it underpins SOAP.

flashcards.front: What is base64 used for, and what is a caveat?
flashcards.back: Encoding binary data (files, certs, images) as ASCII text so it can be embedded in JSON/XML. Caveats: it increases size by ~33% and is encoding, NOT encryption (no confidentiality).

flashcards.front: Why use UTC and ISO 8601 for timestamps?
flashcards.back: Storing/exchanging times in UTC with ISO 8601 (e.g. 2026-06-20T14:30:00Z) avoids timezone and DST ambiguity across systems and regions; convert to local time only for display.

flashcards.front: What is a MIME / media type and where does it appear?
flashcards.back: A label for content kind, e.g. application/json, text/csv, application/pdf. It appears in Content-Type (what is sent) and Accept (what is wanted) headers so the receiver parses correctly.

flashcards.front: Why UTF-8 for an embassy's text data?
flashcards.back: UTF-8 can represent characters from virtually every language/alphabet. Using it consistently prevents corruption (mojibake) of names and addresses in diplomatic records that span many scripts.

title: Messaging, Middleware & Integration Reliability Patterns
summary: Message queues and pub/sub, Kafka semantics, middleware (ESB vs API gateway, EDI), and the reliability patterns — retries, idempotency, circuit breakers, sagas, CDC, versioning — that keep distributed integrations correct.
content:
Asynchronous messaging decouples systems so a sender does not wait for the receiver. A message queue holds messages until a consumer processes them, smoothing load spikes and surviving outages. There are two delivery models. Point-to-point: a message goes to exactly one consumer from a queue (work distribution). Publish/subscribe (pub/sub): a message published to a topic is delivered to all interested subscribers (event broadcast). Choosing async over synchronous trades immediate responses for resilience and scalability.

Apache Kafka is a distributed event-streaming platform. Producers write to topics, which are split into partitions for parallelism. Ordering is guaranteed only within a single partition, not across a topic, so messages that must stay ordered (e.g. all events for one passport number) should share a partition key. Consumers track their offset (position) and can replay history. Delivery semantics matter: at-least-once means no message is lost but duplicates are possible (so consumers must be idempotent); exactly-once is achievable but costly and constrained. Messages that repeatedly fail processing are routed to a dead-letter queue (DLQ) for later inspection instead of blocking the stream.

Middleware sits between systems to mediate communication. An ESB (Enterprise Service Bus) is a centralized hub doing heavy routing, protocol bridging, and message transformation — powerful but can become a bottleneck. An API gateway is a lighter front door for APIs handling authentication, rate limiting, routing, and aggregation. EDI (Electronic Data Interchange) is a long-standing standard (e.g. EDIFACT, X12) for exchanging structured business documents like invoices and customs declarations between organizations — still vital for trade and consular logistics.

• Webhooks vs polling: webhooks push events to you when they happen (efficient, near-real-time) while polling repeatedly asks is there anything new? (simpler but wasteful and laggy).
• Sync vs async: sync blocks awaiting a reply; async hands off work and continues.

Reliability in distributed systems rests on a handful of patterns. Idempotency keys let a client safely retry a non-idempotent operation: the client sends a unique key, and the server processes it only once, returning the original result on retries — preventing duplicate visa charges, for example. Retries should use exponential backoff (increasing delays, ideally with jitter) so a struggling service is not hammered. A circuit breaker stops calling a failing dependency after a threshold of errors, fails fast for a cooldown, then probes for recovery — preventing cascading failures.

Data consistency models differ by system. ACID (Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability) describes strong, transactional guarantees typical of relational databases. BASE (Basically Available, Soft state, Eventual consistency) describes many distributed/NoSQL systems that favor availability and accept eventual consistency — replicas converge over time rather than instantly. A saga manages a long, multi-service transaction as a sequence of local steps, each with a compensating action to undo prior steps if one fails (since a global lock is impractical). Change Data Capture (CDC) streams a database's row-level changes as events so other systems stay in sync without bulk re-exports.

Evolving APIs without breaking consumers requires discipline. API versioning (via URL path /v2, headers, or media type) lets old and new clients coexist. Schema compatibility comes in backward (new server reads old messages), forward (old server tolerates new fields), and full flavors — additive changes are usually safe, removals and renames are breaking. Large collections use pagination (offset/limit or cursor-based) so responses stay bounded. Finally, data mapping/transformation translates between each system's model — field names, units, code lists, date formats — and is where much real integration effort lives.
keyPoints: Point-to-point delivers a message to exactly one consumer; pub/sub broadcasts a topic to all subscribers.
Kafka topics are split into partitions; ordering is guaranteed only within a partition, so use a partition key to keep related events ordered.
At-least-once may duplicate (consumers must be idempotent); exactly-once is possible but costly.
Dead-letter queues capture messages that repeatedly fail so they don't block the stream.
ESB = centralized heavyweight integration hub; API gateway = lightweight API front door (auth, rate limiting, routing); EDI exchanges structured business documents (X12/EDIFACT).
Webhooks push events (efficient, real-time); polling repeatedly pulls (simpler but wasteful/laggy).
Idempotency keys let clients safely retry non-idempotent operations without duplicates.
Retries should use exponential backoff with jitter; circuit breakers fail fast on a sick dependency to prevent cascades.
ACID = strong transactional consistency (relational); BASE = eventual consistency favoring availability (distributed/NoSQL).
A saga sequences local transactions with compensating actions; CDC streams DB changes as events; additive schema changes are safe while removals/renames break consumers.

flashcards.back: Point-to-point: a queue delivers each message to exactly one consumer (work sharing). Pub/sub: a message published to a topic is delivered to all subscribers (event broadcast).

flashcards.front: In Kafka, what is ordering guaranteed across?
flashcards.back: Only within a single partition — not across an entire topic. To keep related events ordered, give them the same partition key so they land in the same partition.
Splitting a topic for parallelism and scalability — multiple consumers can read different partitions concurrently. The trade-off is that total ordering exists only per partition.
At-least-once: no message lost, but duplicates possible — consumers must be idempotent. Exactly-once: each message effectively processed once; achievable but more complex and costly.
A holding queue for messages that repeatedly fail processing. Routing them to a DLQ stops a poison message from blocking the stream and lets operators inspect/replay them later.

An ESB is a centralized, heavyweight hub for routing, protocol bridging, and transformation (can be a bottleneck). An API gateway is a lightweight front door for APIs handling auth, rate limiting, routing, and aggregation.
Electronic Data Interchange — standardized formats (e.g. X12, EDIFACT) for exchanging structured business documents like invoices, purchase orders, and customs declarations between organizations.

Webhooks: the server pushes an event to your endpoint when something happens (efficient, near-real-time). Polling: your client repeatedly asks for changes (simpler but wasteful and introduces latency).

A unique client-supplied identifier for an operation. The server processes a given key only once and returns the original result on retries, so retrying a non-idempotent call (e.g. a payment) won't duplicate it.

flashcards.front: Why use exponential backoff for retries?
flashcards.back: Increasing the delay between retries (often with jitter) avoids hammering an already-struggling service, reduces thundering-herd effects, and gives the dependency time to recover.

flashcards.front: What does a circuit breaker do?
flashcards.back: After errors to a dependency exceed a threshold, it 'opens' and fails fast for a cooldown instead of calling the sick service, then half-opens to probe recovery — preventing cascading failures.

ACID (Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability) = strong, immediate transactional guarantees (relational DBs). BASE (Basically Available, Soft state, Eventual consistency) = favors availability; replicas converge over time (many NoSQL/distributed systems).
A model where, after updates stop, all replicas eventually return the same value — but reads may temporarily see stale data. It trades immediate consistency for availability and scalability.

A way to manage a transaction spanning multiple services as a sequence of local transactions, each with a compensating action to undo previous steps if a later one fails — instead of a single distributed lock.
A technique that streams a database's row-level inserts/updates/deletes as events, letting downstream systems stay synchronized in near-real-time without bulk re-exports.

Backward: a new consumer can read data produced by old producers. Forward: an old consumer can tolerate data from new producers (e.g. ignores unknown fields). Additive changes are usually safe; removals/renames break.

flashcards.front: Why and how do APIs paginate?
flashcards.back: To keep responses bounded for large collections. Offset/limit (page numbers) is simple but can skip/duplicate on changing data; cursor-based pagination uses a stable pointer and handles large, mutating datasets better.

flashcards.front: What is data mapping/transformation in integration?
flashcards.back: Translating between systems' differing data models — field names, units, code/value lists, date formats, structure — so each side understands the other. It's often the bulk of real integration work.
